notes.body: An old province covering much of present-day Chiba and part of Ibaraki. 下 means ‘lower’; 総 belongs to the inherited provincial name Fusa. Together 下総 is the fixed place-name Shimōsa, not a freely composed phrase meaning ‘lower total.’ 国 adds ‘province’ or ‘country.’

葛飾 is an inherited place-name: 葛 means ‘kudzu vine’ and 飾 means ‘to adorn,’ but the place-name does not mean ‘kudzu adornment.’ 郡 means an administrative district; thus the whole expression is ‘Katsushika District.’

真間 is the old place-name Mamma, in present-day Ichikawa, and already a poetic place in the Man’yōshū. The characters 真 ‘true’ and 間 ‘interval’ do not transparently generate the name. 郷 means a rural settlement or district, so 真間の郷 is ‘the settlement of Mamma.’

A masculine personal name. 勝 contributes the idea of victory or excellence; 四郎 is a traditional name element, literally ‘fourth son.’ Together they form a name rather than a description of the man in the sentence.
Here 男 is read をとこ. The formula 勝四郎といふ男 places an as-yet simple figure before us: ‘a man called Katsushirō.’

あり is ‘to exist’ or ‘to be.’ The classical auxiliary けり looks back upon what is narrated. In the opening formula 男ありけり, the two together give ‘there lived a man,’ with the quiet distance of an old tale.
祖 means ‘forebear’ and 父 means ‘father’; together 祖父 is the father one generation behind one’s parent, ‘grandfather.’ The text gives the old native reading おほぢ rather than modern そふ.

notes.gloss: for a long time; from of old
notes.body: 旧 carries the sense of what is old or long established. Read ひさしく here, it modifies 住み: the family had dwelt in this place for a long time.
The continuative form of 住む, ‘to dwell.’ It leaves the movement unfinished and joins the family’s long residence to the possessions and prosperity that follow.
田 is a wet or rice field; 畠 is a dry or upland field. Together 田畠 gathers the two principal kinds of cultivated land into one expression: the household’s farmland as a whole.
A native literary word for ‘many.’ It carries more breadth and less numerical precision than a counted quantity.
主 means ‘master’ or ‘owner.’ With づく it forms a verb meaning to have something attached to an owner or under ownership. Here 田畠あまた主づきて means that many fields belonged to the household.
家 is the household and estate, not merely the building; 豊か means abundant or ample. Together in the phrase 家豊か, the family’s whole material condition is prosperous.

暮し is the continuative form of 暮す, ‘to live’ or ‘pass one’s days.’ ける is the attributive form of the retrospective auxiliary けり because が follows: the narration looks back upon the household’s former prosperity.

The writing joins 生 ‘life, birth, growth’ and 長 ‘to lengthen or grow.’ Their combined idea is maturation, but Akinari gives the old native reading ひととなりて: literally, ‘having become a person,’ hence ‘having grown up.’
かかはる is to concern oneself with or attend to something. The classical negative ぬ makes 物にかかはらぬ ‘not concerning himself with things’: an inborn unconcern with practical matters.
Read さが, 性 means the nature with which a person seems to have been born. It is more inward and fateful here than a momentary habit.

農 means agriculture; 作 means making, working, or cultivation. Together 農作 is agricultural work. Akinari assigns it the native reading なりはひ, ‘livelihood’ or ‘occupation,’ rather than the modern Sino-Japanese reading のうさく.
うたてし is an old adjective for what is disagreeable, lamentable, or repellent. Its attributive form うたてき governs 物: he regarded farming as something hateful.
厭ふ means to dislike, weary of, or shun. With retrospective けり in its attributive form ける, 厭ひける presents the aversion as the settled fact from which the consequence follows.

notes.gloss: as that state continued; under its sway
notes.body: まま is the condition in which something is left or allowed to proceed. Here the dislike of farming is not corrected; the household’s fate follows ‘in accordance with’ that aversion.
A connective adverb whose force depends on context. Here it turns disposition into outcome: ‘and so,’ ‘in the end,’ the once-prosperous house becomes poor.
家 again means the household and estate; 貧しく is ‘poor’ or ‘impoverished.’ The phrase therefore names the decline of the family’s whole condition, not the mere appearance of its house.

なり is ‘to become.’ に is the continuative form of the perfective auxiliary ぬ, and けり looks back upon the completed result. Their sequence gives more than a plain ‘became’: the household had come, finally and completely, into poverty.
